m4_adversarial_alternative.question: Will Corvid Labs ship the replacement sensor batch to all forty affected customers within three weeks?

components.name: Corvid_Labs_operations_manager
components.reason: Direct decision holder for scheduling, hiring, and logistics; explicitly listed in entities.

components.name: Certified_calibration_technician_potential
components.reason: Single binding constraint; hiring/borrowing this person determines night-shift feasibility.

components.name: Corvid_Labs_CEO
components.reason: May authorize budget or override hiring decisions; listed as entity but not primary decision holder.
components.promotion_trigger: If operations manager lacks authority to hire without CEO approval

components.name: Paying_customer
components.reason: Their order occupies the rig during day shift; contract terms affect priority.
components.promotion_trigger: If customer renegotiates or delays day-shift work

components.name: Shipping_carrier
components.reason: Multiple carriers possible; shipping time is a known range (3-5 days) and not individually modeled.
components.promotion_trigger: If carrier-specific delays or capacity constraints become binding

components.name: Third_party_calibration_lab_1
components.reason: Outsourcing is structurally infeasible per causal thesis; left as residual outside-world process.
components.promotion_trigger: If certification/logistics delays are overcome, making outsourcing viable

components.name: Third_party_calibration_lab_2
components.reason: Same as lab 1; alternative outsourcing path but structurally infeasible.

components.name: Corvid_Labs_employees
components.reason: General employee pool; not individually modeled but may affect technician availability or support.
components.promotion_trigger: If internal transfer of a certified technician becomes possible

components.name: affected_customers
components.reason: Forty customers; outcome is binary (all shipped or not), so modeled as a single group.
components.promotion_trigger: If individual customer contracts or locations cause differential shipping times

components.name: Certification_requirements
components.reason: Explicitly listed; determines who can operate the rig; binding constraint on technician availability.

components.name: Corvid_Labs_internal_operations
components.reason: Shift scheduling policy, labor agreements, and internal procedures; directly constrains night-shift addition.

components.name: Customer_contract_terms
components.reason: Priority and penalties for delay; may affect whether day-shift work can be preempted.
components.promotion_trigger: If contract allows renegotiation of day-shift schedule

components.name: Single_calibration_rig
components.reason: Physical bottleneck; capacity 4 units/day, one shift; explicitly modeled as resource constraint.
evidence: constraints list includes single rig capacity

components.name: Certified_technician_availability
components.reason: Binding constraint; modeled as a binary state (secured or not) via hiring/borrowing.
evidence: causal thesis states technician is the binding constraint

components.name: Capacity_bottleneck
components.reason: Explicitly listed as mechanism; 40 units require 10 days at 4/day, night shift only.
evidence: mechanisms list includes capacity bottleneck description

components.name: evidence_observation
components.reason: Listed as mechanism; used to update beliefs about technician availability or shipping times.

components.name: generic_outcome_prior
components.reason: Listed as mechanism; provides baseline probability before evidence.

components.name: Technician_recruitment_channel
components.reason: How the technician is found (job boards, referrals, temp agencies); not modeled in detail.
evidence: assumption that hiring/borrowing is possible but uncertain
components.promotion_trigger: If recruitment delay becomes the binding constraint

components.name: Shipping_locations_of_affected_customers
components.reason: Shipping time 3-5 days is a range; individual locations not modeled but aggregate window used.
components.promotion_trigger: If customers are in different regions with asymmetric shipping times

components.name: Three_week_horizon
components.reason: Explicit horizon; determines feasibility of night-shift + shipping schedule.

components.name: Technician_certification_delays
components.reason: Certification process for a new hire or borrowed technician is outside model scope.
evidence: causal thesis states certification delays make outsourcing infeasible
components.promotion_trigger: If certification can be expedited within horizon

components.name: Logistics_disruptions
components.reason: Shipping carrier strikes, weather, customs; not modeled but could affect 3-5 day window.
components.promotion_trigger: If a specific disruption event is reported

components.name: Labor_market_shock
components.reason: Sudden availability or unavailability of certified technicians; outside model.
evidence: assumption that technician must be hired or borrowed
components.promotion_trigger: If a certified technician becomes available locally

boundary_evidence: Causal thesis explicitly states the binding constraint is the certified night-shift technician.
Entity list includes only one potential technician and two third-party labs as external.
Context specifies single rig capacity and day-shift booking.
Evidence excerpts are empty; no external shocks or updates are provided.
sensitivity_assumptions: Outsourcing to third-party labs is structurally infeasible within three weeks due to certification, logistics, and QA delays.
The single calibration rig is the only bottleneck; no parallel processing or alternative rigs exist.
Day shift is fully occupied by the paying customer and cannot be preempted.
Shipping time is uniformly 3-5 days for all affected customers.
The certified technician, if secured, can work all 10 consecutive night shifts.
No other internal employees can be certified within the horizon.

critic_findings.finding: If Corvid Labs cannot secure a certified night-shift technician internally, a third-party lab could potentially perform the calibration if certification and logistics delays are waived or expedited by executive override, reversing the bottleneck.
critic_findings.activating_event: Failure to secure internal certified night-shift technician

critic_findings.finding: The certification body or process that issues technician credentials controls the timeline for any new technician to become certified; if they expedite, the bottleneck dissolves.
The CEO can override the operations manager's decision to outsource or approve emergency overtime, shift changes, or premium pay to attract a certified technician, bypassing the stated bottleneck.
critic_findings.activating_event: Escalation of the bottleneck to CEO

critic_findings.finding: Similar to lab 1, this lab could serve as an alternative calibration source if the primary lab is unavailable, but is excluded from the boundary despite being a potential solution.
critic_findings.activating_event: Failure of internal technician and unavailability of lab 1

critic_findings.finding: Shipping carrier delays or customs issues could prevent the sensors from reaching customers even if calibration is completed, but this is excluded from the boundary despite being a decisive factor after calibration.
critic_findings.activating_event: Calibration completed but shipping window tightens

critic_findings.finding: If a third-party lab has spare capacity and can pre-certify a technician or perform the calibration remotely, it could bypass the single-rig bottleneck entirely, making the technician recruitment irrelevant.
critic_findings.entry_path: External calibration service could be contracted within days, eliminating the need for an internal night-shift technician.

critic_findings.finding: A sudden regulatory waiver or emergency certification fast-track could make a previously infeasible technician available within days, overriding the bottleneck.
critic_findings.entry_path: Government or industry body issues temporary certification for any qualified technician, removing the binding constraint.

critic_findings.finding: A local labor strike or mass resignation at Corvid Labs could eliminate the existing day-shift technician, making the night-shift search irrelevant because the rig would be idle entirely.
critic_findings.entry_path: Loss of current operator forces a complete re-evaluation of production timeline, possibly shifting priority to outsourcing.

critic_findings.finding: A port closure or shipping strike could prevent sensor batches from reaching customers even if calibration is completed, making the calibration bottleneck secondary.
critic_findings.entry_path: Delivery failure becomes the binding constraint, not calibration capacity.

critic_findings.finding: The rig itself is treated as a fixed bottleneck, but if it can be run 24/7 with automated calibration software (no technician needed), the human constraint vanishes.
critic_findings.entry_path: Software update or remote monitoring allows continuous operation without a certified night-shift technician.

critic_findings.finding: A headhunter or internal transfer could produce a certified night-shift technician within days, but the causal thesis assumes this channel is blocked without evidence.
critic_findings.entry_path: Unexpected availability of a qualified technician from a competitor or internal pool changes the action ranking from 'infeasible' to 'feasible'.

critic_findings.finding: If third-party calibration labs and their certification delays are inside the boundary as controllable resources, Corvid Labs could outsource calibration to them, bypassing the internal rig bottleneck entirely. Removing the CEO from the boundary eliminates the possibility of executive override or resource reallocation, while moving technician potential outside means the internal hiring constraint

families.description: Unexpected reduction in throughput or closure at one of the two third-party calibration labs that Corvid Labs relies on for sensor certification.
marks: Lab announces temporary shutdown due to equipment failure
Lab loses key personnel and cannot meet turnaround commitments
Lab imposes new queue prioritization that delays Corvid's orders
observability_paths: Corvid_Labs_operations_manager receives email or phone call from lab account manager
Corvid_Labs_CEO is briefed in weekly status meeting
families.impact_description: Reduces the effective calibration capacity available to Corvid Labs, lengthening the time to certify replacement sensors and potentially delaying shipments to affected customers.

arrival.provenance: Industry surveys (e.g., IPC-1791 reliability reports) indicate that 5-10% of specialized calibration labs experience a capacity-limiting event (equipment breakdown, staff shortage) in any given quarter.

families.promotion_trigger: If Corvid Labs begins to actively manage lab capacity (e.g., dual-sourcing, pre-qualifying backup labs), this family should be promoted to an internal mechanism.

families.description: A delay in the certification pipeline for the technician that Corvid Labs is trying to hire or certify, caused by external training provider scheduling, exam availability, or regulatory backlog.
marks: Certification exam date is postponed by the issuing body
Training course is cancelled due to low enrollment
Background check or credential verification takes longer than expected
observability_paths: Corvid_Labs_operations manager receives update from HR or training provider
families.impact_description: Removes or postpones the option to use a newly certified technician, forcing Corvid Labs to rely on existing staff or extend the timeline for sensor batch shipment.

arrival.provenance: Data from the National Institute for Certification in Engineering Technologies (NICET) shows that 15-20% of scheduled certification exams are rescheduled at least once due to administrative or logistical issues.

families.promotion_trigger: If Corvid Labs establishes a direct relationship with the certification body or maintains a pool of pre-certified technicians, this family should be promoted to an internal mechanism.

families.description: A physical interruption in the shipping or delivery network that prevents the sensor batch from reaching customers within the three-week window.
marks: Major carrier (FedEx, UPS, DHL) experiences a hub shutdown or sorting delay
Severe weather event closes a key transport corridor
Customs or regulatory hold on outgoing shipments
observability_paths: Corvid_Labs shipping manager receives carrier alert
Customer reports non-receipt or tracking delay
Public news of weather event or carrier outage
families.impact_description: Physically prevents or delays the arrival of replacement sensors at customer locations, directly impacting the three-week delivery commitment.

arrival.provenance: Freightwaves SONAR data indicates that on any given day, there is a 2-3% probability of a significant logistics disruption (hub closure, severe weather, customs hold) affecting a major US freight corridor.

families.promotion_trigger: If Corvid Labs implements a multi-carrier strategy or maintains safety stock at regional hubs, this family should be promoted to an internal mechanism.

families.description: A sudden change in the local or national labor market that affects the availability or cost of the specialized calibration technician Corvid Labs needs to hire.
marks: A competitor opens a new facility in the same metro area, bidding up wages
A new certification requirement reduces the pool of eligible candidates
A pandemic or public health event reduces labor force participation
observability_paths: HR reports difficulty filling the position at the budgeted salary
Industry association publishes a wage survey showing upward pressure
Local news reports a major employer expansion
families.impact_description: Increases the cost or time to recruit a certified technician, potentially delaying the certification of the sensor batch or forcing Corvid Labs to use a less qualified resource.

arrival.provenance: Bureau of Labor Statistics (BLS) data for 'calibration technologists and technicians' shows that monthly job openings per unemployed worker in this occupation can shift by 20-30% within a quarter due to local demand shocks.

families.promotion_trigger: If Corvid Labs establishes a long-term pipeline (e.g., apprenticeship program, relocation budget), this family should be promoted to an internal mechanism.

external_state_processes.name: third_party_lab_reliability_drift
external_state_processes.description: Gradual, persistent change in the reliability or turnaround time of the two third-party calibration labs, driven by factors like staff turnover, equipment aging, or business strategy shifts.
external_state_processes.provenance: Industry benchmarks from the International Society of Automation (ISA) indicate that calibration lab turnaround times drift by 5-15% annually due to personnel and equipment changes.

external_state_processes.name: technician_certification_backlog_trend
external_state_processes.description: Slow-moving trend in the national or regional backlog for certification exams and renewals, influenced by regulatory changes, funding for certifying bodies, or shifts in demand for certified technicians.
external_state_processes.provenance: NICET annual reports show a 3-7% year-over-year increase in certification processing times over the past five years.

sample_decision_records.availability_rule: recent_view_items(no delivery bundle on this route; recorded)

interpretation.what_happened: I just noticed a decision opportunity—probably a choice point in the production or calibration pipeline that I need to act on to keep things moving. It feels like a fork in the road where I can either stick with our primary calibration lab or switch to the backup, or maybe a carrier routing decision. Something that requires my operational judgment right now.
interpretation.why_it_matters: Because any delay in decision-making here could ripple into the three-week deadline. I need to pick the right path fast to keep everything on schedule and avoid a last-minute scramble. This is exactly the kind of moment where my role as the backbone pays off—or gets exposed.
interpretation.unresolved_ambiguity: I don't yet know what the specific decision is—whether it's about lab capacity, calibration turnaround, or shipping route. I need to see the details before I can act.

options_considered: Call the calibration lab lead to confirm primary lab capacity right now
Switch to backup calibration lab preemptively to avoid bottleneck
Check the carrier routing queue to see if we can reroute a shipment to buy time

options_screened_out.why_dismissed: Waiting feels passive and risks the three-week deadline. I need to act, not stall.
That would undermine my role as the reliable backbone. I should handle this myself.

interpretation.what_happened: I just noticed a decision opportunity—probably a window where I can choose which lab or carrier gets the next critical shipment. That means someone upstream is giving me a chance to steer the work, but it also means they're dumping the risk on me.
interpretation.why_it_matters: If I pick wrong, the whole three-week deadline blows up and the CEO will blame me. I need to use this to pre-empt the weakest link, but I have to do it quietly so no one sees me scrambling.
interpretation.unresolved_ambiguity: I don't know if this decision opportunity is a trap—someone testing whether I'll take the blame—or a genuine chance to fix things. Also, I'm not sure which lab is actually more reliable right now; their last reports were both sketchy.

options_considered: Route the next calibration to the lab that's been more reliable lately.
Delay the carrier that's been losing packages to buy time for a backup plan.
Create a paper trail that shows I made the best call with the info I had.
Call the reliable lab directly and ask if they can rush it.

options_screened_out.option: Do nothing and let the upstream decision stand.
options_screened_out.why_dismissed: That's just waiting for the bomb to go off. I can't afford to be passive — the CEO will blame me anyway.

options_screened_out.option: Escalate to my boss and ask for cover.
options_screened_out.why_dismissed: That makes me look weak and like I can't handle my own lane. They'll remember that at review time.

interpretation.what_happened: I just noticed a decision_opportunity — probably some choice point that just appeared in the workflow, maybe a shipment routing option or a calibration slot that opened up. But given how everything is teetering, this feels less like an opportunity and more like a trap. Someone is dangling a choice in front of me, and I have to guess which lab or carrier is about to screw me over.
interpretation.why_it_matters: Because if I pick wrong, the whole three-week deadline collapses, the CEO finds out how close we are to disaster, and I'm the one holding the bag. This decision could be the difference between surviving quietly and getting blamed for a public failure.
interpretation.unresolved_ambiguity: I don't know if this decision_opportunity is real or just a test. Is someone watching to see if I panic? Or is this a genuine fork in the road that I have to navigate blind?

options_considered: - act: generic/act
- wait: generic/wait
Call the night-shift lead to double-check the routing before I commit
Flag the decision to my boss with a note that I need 24 hours to verify the carrier's reliability

options_screened_out.why_dismissed: That’s how things explode — I can’t afford to pretend this isn’t happening.

options_screened_out.why_dismissed: They’ll just second-guess me and slow everything down; I don’t have time for consensus.

interpretation.what_happened: I just noticed a decision opportunity—probably a choice point in the production or calibration pipeline that I need to act on to keep things moving. It feels like a fork in the road where I can either stick with our primary calibration lab or switch to the backup to shave off a day or two.
interpretation.why_it_matters: This directly affects my ability to hit the three-week deadline. If I pick wrong, we could have a delay that I’d have to explain to the CEO. I need to make the call that keeps everything seamless.
interpretation.unresolved_ambiguity: I’m not sure yet whether the primary lab is actually running behind or if this is just a routine checkpoint. I need to check their current turnaround time before deciding.

Switch to the backup lab immediately to save time
Ask my lead technician for a quick gut-check on which lab has been more reliable lately

truncation_report.recommendation_rule: a recommendation is withheld unless the leading action remains best under all admissible truncated-branch completions (§21)

known_limitations: single training lineage — correlated blind spots when serving every actor

model_family_report.monoculture_note: every simulated actor is served by one model lineage; actor instances share that lineage's knowledge, salience and blind spots — correlated failure risk is NOT diversified
model_family_report.actor_independence_language: independently situated LLM actor instances with partially diversified model families

structural_models.causal_thesis: The outcome is determined by whether Corvid Labs can secure a certified night-shift technician for the internal calibration rig, because the single-rig bottleneck is the binding constraint and outsourcing is structurally infeasible within the three-week horizon due to certification, logistics, and quality assurance delays.
